name: Retort Carbon Distillation Learning
executive_summary:
The Retort Carbon Distillation Learning framework maps the thermochemical process of charcoal production onto knowledge distillation in machine learning. In pyrolysis, wood is heated in a low-oxygen retort, driving off water, volatile organic compounds (terpenes, methanol, acetic acid), and tars in a carefully controlled sequence. What remains is a carbon-rich structure that preserves the original wood's cellular geometry -- the tracheid and vessel architecture remains visible under microscopy -- but at a fraction of the original mass. The charcoal burns hotter and cleaner because the energy-diluting volatiles have been removed; however, the process is irreversible. The driven-off compounds cannot be recaptured, and the charcoal lacks the biological functionality (water transport, nutrient storage, pathogen defense) that those volatile compounds provided in living wood.

Knowledge distillation follows this pyrolytic logic with remarkable structural fidelity. A large teacher model (the seasoned wood) is 'heated' through extensive querying across the input distribution, and its soft probability outputs over classes encode the learned topology of the problem space -- which classes the teacher finds similar, where decision boundaries are uncertain, how confidence distributes across the output space. The student model (the charcoal) is trained to reproduce these soft outputs, effectively preserving the teacher's structural geometry (learned decision topology) while shedding the volatile components: redundant parameters, training noise captured in rarely-activated pathways, and capacity allocated to memorizing training-set idiosyncrasies. The resulting student is computationally lighter and often more efficient at inference, just as charcoal is energy-denser per unit mass.

The framework generates specific predictions about distillation design by extending the pyrolysis analogy. Temperature control matters: too-high temperature in charcoal production destroys cellular structure (producingite rather than charcoal), just as too-hard distillation targets (low temperature parameter in softmax) collapse the soft-label information back toward hard labels, losing the uncertainty structure that is the primary value of distillation. The framework also predicts that ensemble teachers -- analogous to blended wood feedstocks -- produce more robust distillation because they average out individual-model idiosyncrasies (species-specific volatiles), leaving a more generalizable structural residue. Most importantly, the irreversibility principle predicts that distilled students will systematically underperform teachers on tasks requiring the 'volatile' capabilities: rare-class discrimination, fine-grained uncertainty calibration, and out-of-distribution detection, because these capabilities depend on the capacity that was driven off.

mapped_components.unit: A single parameter or parameter group in the neural network, analogous to a molecular component in wood. Parameters that contribute to the model's core learned topology (carbon-equivalent structural elements) are retained during distillation; parameters that encode noise, redundancy, or training-set-specific memorization (volatile organic compounds) are driven off.
mapped_components.variation: The diversity of information encoded in the teacher model's soft probability outputs across the input distribution. High-temperature softmax outputs expose more variation (richer volatile spectrum) by revealing the teacher's uncertainty structure and inter-class similarity judgments. Low-temperature outputs suppress this variation, yielding harder labels with less transferable information.
mapped_components.selection: The distillation loss function selects which aspects of the teacher's knowledge are preserved in the student. KL-divergence on soft labels preserves distributional structure (retains cellular geometry); cross-entropy on hard labels discards it (producesite). The student's limited capacity forces selection: only the most consistent, highest-signal patterns survive compression.
mapped_components.fitness: Student model fitness is measured as the ratio of task performance to parameter count (energy density per unit mass). A well-distilled student achieves teacher-competitive accuracy at 5-20x fewer parameters, analogous to charcoal's higher energy density per kilogram compared to raw wood. Fitness degrades on tasks requiring the lost volatile capabilities.
mapped_components.boundary: The framework applies to distillation scenarios where the student has meaningfully fewer parameters than the teacher and the task distribution is broad enough that the teacher develops structured soft-label outputs. It is less applicable to same-architecture self-distillation (no mass reduction) or to narrow classification tasks where soft labels carry minimal additional information beyond hard labels (low-volatile-content wood analogue).

falsifiable_predictions: Student models distilled using soft labels from ensemble teachers (blended feedstock) will show better calibration on out-of-distribution inputs than students distilled from single-teacher soft labels, because ensemble averaging removes teacher-specific idiosyncrasies (species-specific volatiles) while preserving shared structural knowledge.

Distillation with temperature parameter T between 3 and 8 (moderate pyrolysis temperature) will produce students with better OOD calibration than distillation at T=1 (raw hard labels, no pyrolysis) or T>15 (excessive temperature that flattens all distributions, destroying structural information). The optimal T will correlate with task complexity: more complex tasks benefit from higher T, analogous to denser woods requiring higher pyrolysis temperatures.

Student models will show systematically worse performance on tail classes (those with <1% of training data) relative to their performance on head classes, compared to the teacher's head-tail performance ratio. The tail-class degradation will be proportional to the compression ratio (student params / teacher params), because tail-class knowledge is 'volatile' -- encoded in rarely-activated capacity that is preferentially shed.

Progressively distilling through a chain of decreasing-size models (teacher -> medium student -> small student) will preserve more structural information than single-step distillation to the same final size, analogous to staged pyrolysis at increasing temperatures yielding better charcoal than single-stage rapid heating.

Student models distilled from teachers that were themselves distilled will show compounding calibration degradation on uncertainty-sensitive tasks, analogous to re-charring already-charred material: each cycle drives off more of the remaining structural detail, with diminishing returns on efficiency gains.

Feature-map distillation (training the student to match intermediate teacher representations) will preserve more out-of-distribution detection capability than output-only distillation, because intermediate representations encode structural geometry that output-layer soft labels partially obscure -- analogous to preserving internal cellular architecture vs. only matching surface carbonization.

supporting_evidence.finding: Hinton, Vartanian, and Dean (2015) demonstrated that soft targets from a teacher model carry 'dark knowledge' -- information about inter-class similarities and decision boundary geometry that hard labels discard. Students trained on soft targets consistently outperformed those trained on hard labels alone, particularly on classes with limited training data.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Directly validates the core analogy: soft labels are the structural geometry (carbon skeleton) preserved through distillation, while hard labels are the charcoal-equivalent that has lost the volatile information. The 'dark knowledge' concept maps precisely to the cellular geometry preserved in charcoal production.
supporting_evidence.source: Hinton, G., Vinyals, O., and Dean, J. (2015). Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network. arXiv:1503.02531.

supporting_evidence.finding: Tang et al. (2020) showed that ensemble distillation produces better-calibrated student models than single-teacher distillation, with the calibration advantage most pronounced on out-of-distribution and adversarial inputs. The ensemble's averaged soft labels provided a smoother, more generalizable training signal.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Supports the blended-feedstock prediction: ensemble teachers (blended wood) produce distilled students with better generalization properties because teacher-specific noise (species-specific volatiles) is averaged out, leaving a purer structural signal for the student to learn.
supporting_evidence.source: Tang, J. et al. (2020). Understanding and Improving Knowledge Distillation. arXiv:2002.03532.

supporting_evidence.finding: In charcoal science, Byrne and Nagle (1997) demonstrated that pyrolysis temperature directly controls the degree of structural preservation: temperatures of 300-500C preserve recognizable cellular anatomy in the charcoal, while temperatures above 700C cause structural collapse into amorphous carbon. The char yield (mass fraction retained) decreases monotonically with temperature, but energy density peaks at intermediate temperatures.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides the source-domain empirical basis for the temperature-control prediction. The existence of an optimal temperature range that maximizes energy density while preserving structural integrity maps directly onto the distillation temperature parameter's effect on student model quality.
supporting_evidence.source: Byrne, C.E. and Nagle, D.C. (1997). Carbonization of Wood for Advanced Materials Applications. Carbon, 35(2), 259-266.

supporting_evidence.finding: Cho and Hariharan (2019) found that larger teacher-student capacity gaps can paradoxically reduce distillation effectiveness -- very large teachers sometimes produce worse students than moderately-sized teachers. They attributed this to the student's inability to faithfully reproduce the teacher's complex soft-label distribution when the capacity gap is too large.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Maps to the pyrolysis observation that excessively rapid or extreme heating destroys rather than preserves structure. When the compression ratio (capacity gap) is too large, the student cannot preserve the teacher's structural geometry, analogous to high-temperature carbonization producing amorphous carbon rather than structured charcoal.
supporting_evidence.source: Cho, J.H. and Hariharan, B. (2019). On the Efficacy of Knowledge Distillation. ICCV 2019.

experimental_designs.name: Temperature-Controlled Distillation Calibration Study
experimental_designs.description: Train a large teacher model (ResNet-152 or ViT-Large) on ImageNet, then distill to a fixed student architecture (ResNet-18) at 10 different temperature values (T = 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 12, 16, 20, 30, 50). For each temperature, evaluate the student on: (a) in-distribution top-1 accuracy, (b) expected calibration error (ECE) on held-out test set, (c) AUROC for out-of-distribution detection using ImageNet-O and ImageNet-A, (d) tail-class accuracy for classes with fewer than 50 training examples. Plot all metrics against temperature to identify the optimal range and test whether the charcoal analogy's prediction of a peak at intermediate temperatures holds.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: In-distribution accuracy will plateau between T=3 and T=10. ECE and OOD detection will peak between T=4 and T=8 before degrading at higher temperatures. Tail-class accuracy will show the steepest decline at high temperatures (T>15) because high-temperature softmax distributions flatten the fine-grained inter-class distinctions that rare classes depend on. The optimal temperature for calibration will be higher than the optimal temperature for raw accuracy, analogous to higher pyrolysis temperatures producing more energy-dense but structurally simpler charcoal.
experimental_designs.feasibility: High. All components are standard ML research infrastructure. ImageNet training is well-documented, distillation procedures are established, and OOD benchmarks are publicly available. The main cost is GPU compute for training 10 student models, which is modest (approximately 10 GPU-days on a single A100).

experimental_designs.name: Staged vs. Single-Step Distillation Comparison
experimental_designs.description: Compare two distillation pipelines producing the same final student size: (a) single-step distillation from a large teacher (e.g., 300M params) directly to a small student (e.g., 5M params), and (b) staged distillation through two intermediate models (300M -> 80M -> 20M -> 5M). Evaluate both pipelines on in-distribution accuracy, calibration, OOD detection, and performance on compositional generalization benchmarks (SCAN, COGS) that require structural understanding rather than pattern memorization.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Staged distillation will produce students with 2-5% better OOD detection AUROC and 10-20% better compositional generalization accuracy than single-step distillation, despite comparable in-distribution accuracy. This mirrors the charcoal production finding that staged pyrolysis preserves more structural integrity than rapid single-stage carbonization at the same final temperature.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Moderate. Requires training 5 models total (1 teacher, 3 intermediates for staged, 1 direct student) and evaluating on multiple benchmarks. The main challenge is controlling for total training compute: the staged pipeline sees more gradient updates total, so a fair comparison requires equalizing total training FLOPs or demonstrating that the advantage persists when the single-step student receives proportionally more training epochs.

limitations: The irreversibility claim is overstated for ML distillation. Unlike charcoal, a distilled student model can be fine-tuned on additional data to recover some lost capabilities, and the teacher model itself is typically preserved and can be re-queried. The analogy implies a thermodynamic irreversibility that does not strictly apply to information-theoretic compression.

The framework treats all non-essential parameters as equivalent 'volatiles,' but in practice, different types of redundant capacity serve different functions. Some parameters encode regularization effects (preventing overfitting), others encode rare-case coverage, and others are genuinely redundant. Pyrolysis does not distinguish between types of volatiles in the same way, limiting the analogy's prescriptive power for selective distillation.

The temperature parameter in knowledge distillation (softmax scaling) and the temperature in pyrolysis have fundamentally different dimensionalities and dynamics. Pyrolysis temperature is a physical quantity with well-understood thermochemistry; the distillation temperature is a hyperparameter with no underlying physical process. The analogy may generate misleading intuitions about temperature scheduling and dynamics.

Modern distillation techniques (attention transfer, contrastive distillation, self-distillation) go well beyond the simple soft-label transfer that the pyrolysis analogy most naturally maps to. The framework may not generalize cleanly to these more sophisticated methods, which have no obvious charcoal-production counterpart.

The framework assumes that student model capacity is the primary bottleneck, but in practice, training data quality and quantity, learning rate schedules, and architectural design choices often dominate. A well-architected small model trained from scratch on abundant data may outperform a distilled student, undermining the premise that distillation (pyrolysis) is the optimal path to compact models.

connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Both frameworks model irreversible phase transitions driven by removal of stabilizing components. In liquefaction, the stabilizing grain contacts are lost; in pyrolysis, the volatile compounds are driven off. Both transitions are difficult to reverse and produce systems with fundamentally different properties despite unchanged composition at the element level.

Knowledge distillation and firefly synchronization both involve information transfer from a complex system to a simpler one. Distillation transfers learned topology from teacher to student through soft labels; firefly synchronization transfers phase information through light pulses. Both achieve emergent coordination (accurate inference / synchronized flashing) through local information coupling rather than centralized control.

Distilled models may exhibit attentional divergence from their teachers: the student's reduced capacity forces it to attend to different features than the teacher, potentially missing or overweighting certain input dimensions. ADT would predict that distilled models systematically diverge from teacher attention patterns on out-of-distribution inputs where the lost capacity was most needed.
